hero.title: Data-Driven Organizational Diagnostics
hero.subtitle: Identify the root causes of your organizational challenges with the Organizational Alignment Map (OAM) framework — 12 Drivers, 27 Failure Points, 6 Execution Gaps.

intelligence.title: Intelligence
intelligence.desc: Self-diagnose online survey based on the OAM framework

diagnostic.title: Diagnostic
diagnostic.desc: Full diagnosis: Survey + Interview + FGD + Workshop

solution.title: Solution
solution.desc: Precision interventions tailored to diagnosis gaps

ldaas.title: LDaaS
ldaas.desc: Routine health check and ongoing consultation retainer

survey.title: Organizational Alignment Map Survey

intro.anonymous: This survey is fully anonymous. Your responses will be aggregated with other respondents.
intro.instructions: Please answer honestly based on your experience in the organization.

survey.openEndedHelper: Optional. Share specific examples or details.

thankyou.title: Thank You!
thankyou.message: Your responses have been saved anonymously.
thankyou.subtext: Aggregate results will appear on the organization dashboard once the minimum respondent count is reached.

error.submitFailed: Failed to submit. Please try again.

dashboard.title: Organization Dashboard
dashboard.minNotMet: Dashboard activates after at least {{min}} respondents complete the survey.

D1.Q1: I can describe the organization's current direction in simple terms without having to guess.
D1.Q2: The current organizational strategy is clear enough for me to understand as actionable change, not just slogans or general terms.
D1.Q3: When facing many work demands, I know which to prioritize because the organizational priority order is clear.
D1.Q4: Targets or work focus given to me/my team are clearly connected to broader organizational goals.
D1.Q5: The organization's direction and strategy have been translated into daily work, so I know specifically what to do in my role.
D1.Q6: Changes in direction, priorities, or work targets are usually explained clearly enough to avoid different interpretations.
D1.openEnded: Which part of the organization's direction, strategy, priorities, or targets is currently most confusing in work practice? Provide concrete examples.

D2.Q1: I clearly understand how the organization's strategy translates into my team's work direction.
D2.Q2: I know what real changes should occur in my team as an impact of the organizational strategy.
D2.Q3: Understanding of organizational direction and priorities in my team is not significantly different from other teams or units.
D2.Q4: The organizational strategy genuinely affects my team's daily work, not just stopping at the management level.
D2.openEnded: Which part of the strategy cascade to team level is most confusing or unclear? Provide concrete examples.

D3.Q1: Leaders in this organization convey direction and priorities that are aligned with one another.
D3.Q2: I rarely receive conflicting directives from different leaders.
D3.Q3: Messages and decisions conveyed by leadership are relatively consistent over time.
D3.Q4: When direction or decisions change, leaders explain those changes clearly enough to the team.
D3.openEnded: In which area does leadership most often feel misaligned or inconsistent? Provide concrete situational examples.

D4.Q1: I clearly understand my role and responsibilities in daily work.
D4.Q2: In my team, there is rarely confusion about who is responsible for a task.
D4.Q3: Task division in my team is clear and there's rarely overlap or gaps in responsibility.
D4.Q4: When cross-functional work or issues arise, it remains clear who should take responsibility.
D4.openEnded: In your experience, where does confusion about roles or responsibilities most often occur? Provide concrete situational examples.

D5.Q1: I clearly understand who has the authority to make decisions in my work situations.
D5.Q2: Decisions are usually made at the appropriate level without needing to escalate higher.
D5.Q3: Decisions made are generally clear and understandable to those involved.
D5.Q4: Decisions rarely need to be redone or revised due to mistakes or unclarity.
D5.Q5: Decisions can be made quickly enough without protracted processes.
D5.Q6: The decision-making process doesn't involve too many unnecessary parties.
D5.openEnded: In your experience, where does the decision-making process most often face obstacles or fail to run effectively? Provide concrete examples.

D6.Q1: The KPIs or targets I receive have a clear connection to organizational goals.
D6.Q2: Targets in my team are aligned with organization-level targets and don't conflict.
D6.Q3: Team performance is monitored regularly using clear indicators.
D6.Q4: When performance falls short of target, there's clear follow-up based on data or monitoring results.
D6.Q5: Each KPI has a clear owner known to all relevant parties.
D6.Q6: The existing system ensures accountability for KPI achievement, not just relying on individuals.
D6.openEnded: Where is the current KPI system or performance monitoring most unclear or ineffective? Provide concrete examples.

D7.Q1: When targets or KPIs aren't met, there's usually clear follow-up to fix the situation.
D7.Q2: Problems or failures at work aren't allowed to recur without real improvement.
D7.Q3: Leaders actively follow up on team performance, not just setting targets.
D7.Q4: Leaders show clear responsibility for team or organizational results.
D7.Q5: There are clear consequences or actions when performance doesn't match targets.
D7.openEnded: What typically happens when targets are not met in your organization? Provide concrete examples.

D8.Q1: Important information I need to work is usually available on time.
D8.Q2: Information conveyed in the organization rarely changes or is distorted when forwarded.
D8.Q3: I clearly understand messages or information conveyed by other parties.
D8.Q4: Miscommunication rarely causes work to be redone or fixed.
D8.openEnded: What types of information are most often delayed, unclear, or cause miscommunication? Provide concrete examples.

D9.Q1: Priorities between teams or divisions in this organization are generally aligned and don't often clash.
D9.Q2: Working with other teams or divisions can be done smoothly without significant obstacles.
D9.Q3: Inter-team dependencies (handovers, approvals, etc.) are managed well and rarely become bottlenecks.
D9.Q4: When inter-team conflicts arise, they're usually resolved constructively.
D9.Q5: Inter-team conflicts aren't allowed to drag on or recur without clear resolution.
D9.openEnded: What's the biggest challenge in working across teams or divisions currently? Provide concrete situational examples.

D10.Q1: My team has sufficient capability to perform work without significant difficulty.
D10.Q2: Work is rarely delayed or problematic due to skill limitations within the team.
D10.Q3: Existing training or development programs help genuinely improve work performance.
D10.Q4: After training, visible changes occur in the team's work approach or results.
D10.Q5: Skills required for current work can be identified clearly enough.
D10.openEnded: Where do skill or training limitations most often become obstacles in work? Provide concrete examples.

D11.Q1: Team members usually solve problems without always waiting for direction from above.
D11.Q2: When problems arise, the involved individuals tend to take responsibility through to completion.
D11.Q3: Rarely is there blame-shifting in daily work.
D11.Q4: The team actively proposes ideas or improvements without being asked.
D11.Q5: Individuals in the team don't just execute tasks but also strive to improve work methods.
D11.openEnded: In your experience, when did you last see strong or weak ownership or initiative in the team? Describe the situation.

D12.Q1: Feedback is given regularly and specifically enough to help improve work.
D12.Q2: Feedback given is usually genuinely used to improve work methods.
D12.Q3: After errors or problems occur, clear evaluation takes place.
D12.Q4: Real improvements happen after evaluation, not just discussion.
D12.Q5: The same problems don't often recur because previous improvements have been made.
D12.openEnded: How does your organization typically learn from mistakes or feedback? Provide concrete situational examples.
